lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Things have fall’n out, sir, so unluckily,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: That we have had no time to move our daughter:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Look you, she loved her kinsman Tybalt dearly,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And so did I:—Well, we were born to die.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: ’Tis very late, she’ll not come down to-night:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I promise you, but for your company,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I would have been a-bed an hour ago.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: These times of woe afford no time to woo.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Madam, good night: commend me to your daughter.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: I will, and know her mind early to-morrow;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: To-night she is mew’d up to her heaviness.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Sir Paris, I will make a desperate tender

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Of my child’s love: I think she will be ruled

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: In all respects by me; nay, more, I doubt it not.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Wife, go you to her ere you go to bed;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Acquaint her here of my son Paris’ love;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And bid her, mark you me, on Wednesday next—

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: But, soft! what day is this?

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Monday, my lord,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Monday! ha, ha! Well, Wednesday is too soon,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: O’ Thursday let it be: o’ Thursday, tell her,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: She shall be married to this noble earl.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Will you be ready? do you like this haste?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: We’ll keep no great ado—a friend or two;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For, hark you, Tybalt being slain so late,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: It may be thought we held him carelessly,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Being our kinsman, if we revel much:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Therefore we’ll have some half a dozen friends,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And there an end. But what say you to Thursday?

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: My lord, I would that Thursday were to-morrow.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Well, get you gone: o’ Thursday be it, then.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Go you to Juliet ere you go to bed,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Prepare her, wife, against this wedding-day.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Farewell, my lord. Light to my chamber, ho!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Afore me! it is so very very late,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: That we may call it early by and by.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Good night.

stage_directions.text: Enter Capulet, Lady Capulet, and Paris.

glosses.definition: shut up with her grief — 'mew' is a falconer's cage
a rash offer — Capulet pledges Juliet's hand without consulting her
Capulet intends a modest funeral-adjacent wedding

annotations.title: Capulet sets the wedding date — without consulting Juliet
annotations.body: This brief thirty-seven-line scene is the play's most economical turning point. Capulet, in his grief over Tybalt, promises Juliet's hand to Paris for Thursday — two days away. Juliet has just been married to Romeo; Capulet doesn't know. The haste is meant to console — 'o' Thursday let it be: o' Thursday, tell her, / She shall be married to this noble earl' — but it creates the pressure under which Juliet will take the Friar's potion. The scene is important because it happens offstage from Juliet's perspective: when we cut to her in 3.5, the Paris-marriage decision has already been made without her being consulted. Structurally, this is the Elizabethan domestic machine visible in miniature.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: What does Capulet promise Paris in this scene, without consulting Juliet?
options: He will give Paris a gold chain
He promises Juliet's hand in marriage for Thursday — two days away — setting the tragic timeline
He will end the feud with the Montagues
